title: At Least to Keep Their Posts, They Will Spring into Action!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored from the printed page, and clear OCR word-break, spacing, and punctuation errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: With the onset of the northeast monsoon, the heavy rain of the past week has severely disrupted normal life across Tamil Nadu. The livelihoods of people at the bottom have been shattered, every route to earning a living has been closed, and they sit in anguish, not knowing what to do. In Chennai, incessant rain has left low-lying residential areas and hutments struggling in water. Across Tamil Nadu, including the delta districts, crops on several lakh acres lie submerged and rotting. Except for one or two, neither the Chief Minister, the other ministers, nor officials have visited the flooded areas or shown concern for relief work. They are all spending their time fulfilling “votive obligations.”

In the Cauvery delta districts of Thanjavur, Tiruvarur and Nagapattinam, samba and thaladi paddy on six lakh acres has gone under water. Twelve lakh acres had been cultivated. Six lakh acres, transplanted mechanically only twenty days earlier, is now completely submerged. In Karur district, the release of surplus water into the Amaravathi flooded the Pasupathipalayam causeway and stopped traffic. Across Tiruvarur district, including Kottur union and Tiruthuraipoondi, water surrounds two lakh acres of crops.

P. R. Pandian, coordinator of Tiruvarur district's farmers' associations, has said with anguish that most fields in irrigated areas are flooded and that the Public Works Department must accept full responsibility. Farmers repeatedly identified places needing desilting and maintenance, but nothing was done. Keevalur Dhanapal, general secretary of the Cauvery Farmers Protection Association, says sixty per cent of Nagapattinam district's crops are surrounded by rainwater and asks that funds be provided so farmers can reinvest.

Flooding has completely disrupted transport in Vedaranyam, Mayiladuthurai and Sirkazhi. Five thousand fishermen from ten villages, including Vedaranyam and Kodiyakarai, have not gone to sea. Six thousand fishermen in Sethubavachatram, Thanjavur district, have also remained ashore, deprived of their livelihood.

In Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi and Kanniyakumari districts, thousands of acres of planted paddy have been devastated. In Ambasamudram, Tenkasi, Kadayam and Kadayanallur, paddy ready for harvest has been damaged and begun sprouting again, plunging thousands of farmers into distress. Former Radhapuram DMK MLA M. Appavu has written asking me to press for proper relief. He recalls that when harvest-ready crops suffered similar damage under the DMK Government between 2006 and 2011, farmers immediately received Rs 5,000 per acre.

During the previous rains, the northern bank of Tiruvarur's Kamalalayam collapsed. When repairs dragged on under this regime, I offered funds from my constituency development allocation. Officials said the Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments Department, and later the Tourism Department, would call tenders and complete the work quickly. Nothing has been completed to this day. Now the upper bank too has collapsed. Perhaps the Government is “repairing” it in some other fashion: we hear that prohibitory orders under Section 144 have been imposed so that no one can even go there!

Heavy rain has fallen intermittently in Chennai and its suburbs since the seventeenth. Roads in Guindy, Egmore, Mylapore, Velachery, Triplicane, Perambur, Tiruvottiyur and Tiruvanmiyur are full of potholes, severely disrupting traffic. Newspapers have published photographs of damaged roads across the city, including the road at Ripon Buildings. Motorists and pedestrians say flooding on major roads is caused by the failure to maintain storm-water drains. Sewage channels on the road to Vyasarpadi lie open in many places. Vehicles remain trapped for hours, unable either to move forward or turn back. Schoolchildren spend their time merely trying to discover whether school is open or a holiday has been declared.

Power cuts last for hours, whether because of rain or the electricity shortage. In Triplicane, Royapuram, Chepauk and Tondiarpet, sewage mixing with rainwater has created a public-health hazard. Fifteen people suffering vomiting and diarrhoea have been admitted to hospital. Malaria, encephalitis and dengue are reportedly spreading rapidly, along with a mysterious fever that causes severe body and joint pain and leaves its victims incapacitated. The Corporation's health department, which should control disease, remains indifferent.

Social activists ask what happened to the “cold mix” purchased for Rs 87 lakh to repair rain-damaged Chennai roads, a modern concrete mixture that dries quickly even during rain. It has been used for appearance's sake in a few places, but where is the rest?

When DMK members tried to raise the extensive rain damage in the Corporation Council, the Mayor expelled all of them, following the style now routinely practised in the Tamil Nadu Assembly. They were not even allowed to ask why a Chennai Corporation press release omitted current Chief Minister Panneerselvam's name and instead said that work was being done under the guidance of the “People's Chief Minister, Revolutionary Leader.”

Tamil newspapers have criticised the State Government's lethargy and the Chennai Corporation's indifference and inaction. Today's Times of India carries the headline, “Corporation pats itself as City battles rain woes.” It says that while a week of heavy rain had badly disrupted normal life, Saturday's Corporation meeting entirely avoided discussing the damage and instead celebrated three years of achievements. Like Nero playing the fiddle while Rome burned, the Mayor celebrated his feats while the city's people struggled with countless problems.

The delta normally receives an average of 24.64 centimetres of rain in October, but reports say 32.96 centimetres has already fallen this year. Amid this downpour, three seventeen-year-old students who went to bathe in the Marina sea on Deepavali were swept away and killed.

Large quantities of water remain in Sholinganallur, Karapakkam, Okkiyam Thoraipakkam, Mettukuppam and Perungudi. Floodwater surrounded Karapakkam's Gangai Amman temple, preventing worship for two days. At Menambedu in Ambattur, pits dug for underground drainage remained uncovered, disappeared beneath rainwater, and endangered road users.

When DMK colleagues, especially legislators and our treasurer, brother Stalin, visit flooded areas, officials who should accompany them and help solve people's problems reportedly run and hide out of fear of the ruling party.

Only after parties across the spectrum repeatedly complained that the Government neither cared about the extensive damage nor carried out relief work did the AIADMK regime experience a sudden enlightenment. In haste, it sent thirty-one IAS officers to inspect flood damage and ordered them to tour entire districts and report in a single day. Their assessments are to be used to calculate statewide damage and seek relief funds from the Union Government. But what will the State Government itself do for the victims? How much relief will it provide? It says nothing, concentrating only on obtaining funds from Delhi. The rulers seem to have forgotten that immediate relief is crucial to people struck by rain and flood.

In much of Dindigul district, particularly Palani and nearby villages, people have lost their homes and taken shelter in public places. The damage is said to be unprecedented. District minister Natham Viswanathan has not visited the affected places or supervised relief. People waited after being told that Chief Minister Panneerselvam would visit. He came as far as Madurai, but returned to Chennai without concerning himself with the flood damage.

Continuous rain around Salem has exposed the public to various diseases. More than five thousand people are receiving outpatient treatment at Salem Government Hospital and 140 have been admitted. Fever is spreading rapidly in Tiruchi, Tirunelveli and Palani too. Tamil Nadu is turning into a “land of fever,” yet the Government that should plan and implement vigorous preventive action remains inert.

For a week, rainwater in Tirunelveli has flowed wastefully into the Thamirabarani. Even when farmers cried in tears before Deepavali, “The tank is about to breach; officials, come and save us,” no one came. Only after it breached and Uthumalai village floated in water did officials arrive, and villagers surrounded them. Villagers likewise surrounded a ruling-party MLA who visited badly affected Kombaipatti near Palani. The ruling party's neglect is provoking public anger.

A. Viyanarasu of the Thamirabarani Irrigation Rights Protection Council says that because of Public Works Department negligence, instead of diverting water into agricultural tanks, rainwater is flowing uselessly into the sea at ten thousand cubic feet per second. Five TMC has already reached the sea without benefiting anyone. Had the Tirunelveli river-link canal project been implemented, the water could have been diverted there. The present Government has neglected even that project begun under the DMK administration.

I have tried to bring together the suffering inflicted across Tamil Nadu by ten days of heavy rain. At least to demonstrate that a government exists in this State, its rulers must free themselves from fear, immediately summon all District Collectors, and accelerate essential flood-relief work. They must not merely wait for the Union Government. The State has a separate special fund for this purpose. If the Finance Secretary is consulted, an initial relief allocation can be announced and released within a day or two.

The Government must also announce how many thousands of rupees per acre will be paid to affected farmers. Each minister must immediately be sent to a district to meet and console the victims and accelerate measures to protect them. Our people must not be abandoned at this difficult hour.

Panneerselvam may, without “fear,” issue a statement to the newspapers unfailingly declaring that all these measures are being carried out at the insistence of the “People's Chief Minister, Revolutionary Leader, Amma.” Only then, perhaps, will the post he now holds remain secure!
With affection,
M.K.
